title: Introduction to Academic Skills
content:
Introduction to Academic Skills

University study requires a different set of skills from school or college. You will be expected to take greater responsibility for your own learning, think critically about sources, and produce work that demonstrates depth of understanding. This module covers the essential academic skills you need to succeed at university.

Learning Objectives

  • Understand the expectations of university-level study
  • Develop effective note-taking and reading strategies
  • Plan and structure academic essays
  • Manage your time effectively

Key Concepts

University learning is built on independent study, critical engagement with sources, and academic integrity. You will need to develop skills in information literacy, academic writing, and reflective practice. The transition to university can be challenging, but with the right strategies you can succeed.
subject: Academic Skills

title: Critical Reading Strategies
content:
Critical Reading Strategies

Critical reading is the foundation of academic study. It involves actively engaging with texts rather than passively absorbing information. You need to question what you read, evaluate the evidence presented, and form your own judgement.

Key Techniques

  • Previewing: Scan the text before reading to understand its structure and main arguments
  • Annotating: Make notes in the margins, highlight key points, and question the author
  • Summarising: Write brief summaries of each section in your own words
  • Questioning: Ask who wrote it, why, for whom, and what evidence supports their claims

The SQ3R Method

Survey the text → Question what you want to learn → Read actively → Recite key points from memory → Review your notes regularly.
subject: Academic Skills

title: Note-Taking Methods
content:
Note-Taking Methods

Effective note-taking is essential for university success. Different situations call for different methods. Experiment to find what works best for you.

Methods

  • Cornell Method: Divide your page into cues (left), notes (right), and summary (bottom). Great for lectures and revision.
  • Mind Mapping: Start with a central concept and branch out. Ideal for brainstorming and seeing connections.
  • Outline Method: Use headings and bullet points in a hierarchical structure. Good for well-structured content.
  • Charting Method: Create tables to compare and contrast information. Useful for subjects with lots of data.

Tips

Always date your notes. Leave space to add more later. Review and revise your notes within 24 hours. Use your own words — don't just copy verbatim.
subject: Academic Skills

title: Essay Planning and Structure
content:
Essay Planning and Structure

A well-structured essay is clear, logical, and persuasive. Planning is essential before you start writing.

The Essay Structure

  • Introduction (10-15%): Hook, context, thesis statement, roadmap
  • Body paragraphs (70-80%): Each paragraph makes one clear point, supported by evidence and analysis
  • Conclusion (10-15%): Synthesis of arguments, implications, limitations, future research

PEEL Paragraph Structure

Point — Make your claim. Evidence — Support with sources. Explanation — Analyse the evidence. Link — Connect to your thesis or next paragraph.
subject: Academic Skills

title: Academic Tone and Style
content:
Academic Tone and Style

Academic writing has specific conventions. Mastering these will help you communicate your ideas effectively.

Key Features

  • Formal language: Avoid contractions (don't → do not), colloquialisms, and slang
  • Third person: Use 'this study found' rather than 'I found' (unless reflective writing)
  • Evidence-based: Every claim should be supported by a source or data
  • Cautious language (hedging): Use 'suggests', 'indicates', 'may', 'could' rather than absolute statements
  • Precision: Be specific about what you mean. Avoid vague terms like 'things' or 'stuff'

Common Mistakes

Overusing long words, writing overly complex sentences, and failing to define key terms. Clarity is more important than complexity.
subject: Academic Skills

title: Harvard Referencing
content:
Harvard Referencing

Referencing is how you acknowledge the sources you have used. Harvard is the most common referencing style at UK universities.

In-Text Citations

Use (Author, Year) format. For two authors: (Author and Author, Year). For three or more: (Author et al., Year). Include page numbers for direct quotes: (Author, Year, p. 12).

Reference List

  • Book: Author, A. (Year) Title. Edition. Place: Publisher.
  • Journal article: Author, A. (Year) 'Article title', Journal Name, Volume(Issue), pp. pages.
  • Website: Organisation (Year) Page title. Available at: URL (Accessed: date).

Key Rules

List alphabetically by author surname. Use hanging indentation. Be consistent. Use a reference manager like Zotero or EndNote.
subject: Academic Skills

title: Introduction to Critical Thinking
content:
Introduction to Critical Thinking

Critical thinking is the ability to think clearly and rationally about what to do or what to believe. It is one of the most important skills for university study.

Core Skills

  • Analysis: Breaking down information into its component parts
  • Evaluation: Assessing the strength of arguments and evidence
  • Inference: Drawing logical conclusions from available information
  • Explanation: Articulating your reasoning clearly
  • Self-regulation: Reflecting on your own thinking processes

Why It Matters

Employers consistently rank critical thinking as one of the most desirable skills. It helps you make better decisions, avoid being misled, and construct stronger arguments.
subject: Critical Thinking

title: Analysing Arguments
content:
Analysing Arguments

An argument is a set of claims where some claims (premises) are offered as reasons to accept another claim (the conclusion).

Identifying Arguments

  • Premises: The evidence or reasons given to support a conclusion. Look for words like 'because', 'since', 'as', 'given that'.
  • Conclusion: The main claim being argued for. Look for words like 'therefore', 'thus', 'so', 'consequently'.
  • Assumptions: Unstated beliefs that connect premises to conclusion. These are often the most interesting part to critique.

Evaluating Arguments

Ask: Are the premises true? Do the premises support the conclusion? Are there any hidden assumptions? Is the reasoning valid?
subject: Critical Thinking

title: Logical Fallacies
content:
Logical Fallacies

Logical fallacies are errors in reasoning that make an argument invalid or unsound. Recognising them helps you evaluate arguments critically.

Common Fallacies

  • Ad hominem: Attacking the person rather than their argument
  • Straw man: Misrepresenting an argument to make it easier to attack
  • False dichotomy: Presenting only two options when more exist
  • Slippery slope: Arguing that one step will inevitably lead to extreme outcomes
  • Circular reasoning: Using the conclusion as evidence for itself
  • Appeal to authority: Using an authority figure as evidence without supporting reasoning
  • Hasty generalisation: Drawing a conclusion from insufficient evidence
subject: Critical Thinking

title: Evaluating Sources
content:
Evaluating Sources

Not all sources are equally reliable. You need to evaluate each source critically before using it in your academic work.

The CRAAP Test

  • Currency — When was it published? Is it up to date for your topic?
  • Relevance — Does it relate to your research question? Is it at the right level?
  • Authority — Who wrote it? What are their credentials? Are they respected in the field?
  • Accuracy — Is the information reliable? Is it peer-reviewed? Are there references?
  • Purpose — Why was it written? Is there bias? Is it trying to inform, persuade, or sell?

Types of Sources

Peer-reviewed journal articles are the most reliable. Books from academic publishers are also trustworthy. Websites and news articles vary — check the source carefully.
subject: Critical Thinking

title: Introduction to Research Methods
content:
Introduction to Research Methods

Research methods are the techniques used to collect and analyse data. Understanding research methods is essential for conducting your own research and evaluating the research of others.

Two Main Approaches

  • Quantitative research: Deals with numbers and statistics. Used to test hypotheses, measure variables, and identify patterns. Methods include surveys, experiments, and structured observations.
  • Qualitative research: Deals with words, meanings, and experiences. Used to explore complex phenomena in depth. Methods include interviews, focus groups, and case studies.

Mixed Methods

Many researchers combine quantitative and qualitative approaches to get a more complete picture. This is called mixed methods research.
subject: Research Methods

title: Data Collection Methods
content:
Data Collection Methods

Choosing the right data collection method is crucial for your research. Each method has strengths and limitations.

Common Methods

  • Surveys and questionnaires: Good for collecting large amounts of data. Can include closed questions (quantitative) and open questions (qualitative).
  • Interviews: Structured (fixed questions), semi-structured (flexible), or unstructured (conversational). Rich, detailed data but time-consuming.
  • Focus groups: Group discussion on a topic. Good for exploring attitudes and social norms.
  • Observations: Watching and recording behaviour. Can be participant (researcher joins in) or non-participant.
  • Document analysis: Analysing existing documents, reports, or media.
subject: Research Methods

title: Research Ethics
content:
Research Ethics

Ethical considerations are fundamental to all research involving human participants. Universities have strict ethical approval processes.

Key Principles

  • Informed consent: Participants must understand what they are agreeing to and give voluntary consent
  • Confidentiality: Protecting participants' identities and personal data
  • Right to withdraw: Participants can leave the study at any time without penalty
  • Avoiding harm: Minimising physical, psychological, and emotional risk
  • Debriefing: Explaining the true purpose of the research after participation

Ethical Approval

Most research requires approval from a university ethics committee. This process ensures your research meets ethical standards before it begins.
subject: Research Methods

title: Professional Communication
content:
Professional Communication

Effective communication is essential in professional settings. This includes verbal, written, and digital communication.

Key Skills

  • Verbal communication: Presenting ideas clearly, active listening, asking questions, and responding appropriately
  • Written communication: Professional emails, reports, and documentation. Clear, concise, and appropriate tone.
  • Digital communication: Using professional tools for virtual meetings, collaborative documents, and project management
  • Non-verbal communication: Body language, eye contact, and professional presence

Email Etiquette

Use a clear subject line. Address the recipient appropriately. Be concise. Use professional language. Proofread before sending. Include a signature with your contact details.
subject: Professional Practice

title: Teamwork and Collaboration
content:
Teamwork and Collaboration

Working effectively in teams is a key skill for university group projects and professional life.

Effective Teamwork

  • Clear roles: Everyone should understand their responsibilities
  • Communication: Regular updates, active listening, and constructive feedback
  • Conflict resolution: Addressing disagreements professionally and finding solutions
  • Shared goals: Working towards common objectives

Team Roles (Belbin)

Different team members naturally take on different roles: Coordinator, Shaper, Plant (ideas person), Monitor-Evaluator, Implementer, Completer-Finisher, Teamworker, Resource Investigator, Specialist. Understanding these helps teams work more effectively.
subject: Professional Practice

title: Reflective Practice
content:
Reflective Practice

Reflective practice is the process of thinking about your experiences to learn from them. It is widely used in professional education and development.

Gibbs' Reflective Cycle

  1. Description: What happened?
  2. Feelings: What were you thinking and feeling?
  3. Evaluation: What was good and bad about the experience?
  4. Analysis: What sense can you make of the situation?
  5. Conclusion: What else could you have done?
  6. Action Plan: What will you do next time?

Tips for Reflective Writing

Be honest and open. Use specific examples. Show what you learned. Demonstrate how you will apply this learning in the future. Use reflective models to structure your writing.
subject: Professional Practice

title: Digital Literacy for University
content:
Digital Literacy for University

Digital literacy is the ability to use digital tools effectively and responsibly for learning, research, and communication.

Key Skills

  • Online research: Using academic databases, advanced search techniques, and evaluating online sources
  • Data analysis: Using spreadsheets for data organisation, basic statistics, and creating visualisations
  • Presentations: Creating effective slides, using visual aids, and public speaking
  • Digital collaboration: Using shared documents, project management tools, and virtual meeting platforms

Digital Safety

Protect your data with strong passwords. Be aware of phishing scams. Understand your digital footprint. Follow data protection regulations (GDPR).
subject: Digital Literacy

title: Time Management
content:
Time Management

Effective time management is essential for balancing multiple assignments, reading, and personal commitments at university.

Strategies

  • Prioritisation: Use the Eisenhower Matrix — urgent/important, not urgent/important, urgent/not important, not urgent/not important
  • Planning: Use a planner or digital calendar. Block out time for reading, writing, and revision.
  • Pomodoro Technique: Work in focused 25-minute blocks, then take a 5-minute break. After 4 Pomodoros, take a longer break.
  • Avoiding procrastination: Break large tasks into smaller steps. Set specific goals. Remove distractions.

Creating a Study Schedule

Map out your semester. Note all assignment deadlines. Work backwards from each deadline to plan your time. Include buffer time for unexpected delays.
subject: Academic Skills

title: Presentations and Public Speaking
content:
Presentations and Public Speaking

Presenting your work is a key skill at university and in professional life. Good preparation and practice can help you feel confident.

Structure

  • Introduction: Tell them what you're going to tell them — outline your talk
  • Body: Tell them — present your main points with evidence
  • Conclusion: Tell them what you told them — summarise key points

Tips

Know your audience. Practice your timing. Use slides as prompts, not scripts. Make eye contact. Speak clearly and at a measured pace. Prepare for questions.
subject: Academic Skills

title: Exam Preparation Strategies
content:
Exam Preparation Strategies

Effective exam preparation starts well before the exam date. Use active revision techniques rather than passive reading.

Active Revision Techniques

  • Active recall: Test yourself without looking at your notes. This strengthens memory more than re-reading.
  • Spaced repetition: Review material at increasing intervals. Use flashcards or apps like Anki.
  • Elaboration: Explain concepts in your own words. Connect new information to what you already know.
  • Practice questions: Do past papers and practice questions under timed conditions.

Exam Day Tips

Read all questions carefully. Plan your time. Start with questions you feel confident about. Leave time to review your answers.
subject: Academic Skills
